epigraph.text: Let your yes be yes and your no be no.
epigraph.quote: The margin of error quantifies our uncertainty about a proportion.

content.text:
For categorical data, we estimate population proportions using sample 
proportions.

content.title: Confidence Interval for Proportion
content.content:
For large samples (when $n\phat \geq 10$ and $n(1-\phat) \geq 10$):
\[
 \phat \pm z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\phat(1-\phat)}{n}}
\]
is an approximate $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $p$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Poll Results
content.problem:
In a poll of 600 voters, 324 support a candidate. Find a 95\% CI 
for the true proportion of supporters.
content.solution:
$\phat = 324/600 = 0.54$, $n = 600$.

Check: $n\phat = 324 \geq 10$ and $n(1-\phat) = 276 \geq 10$. 

SE: $\sqrt{\frac{0.54 \times 0.46}{600}} = \sqrt{0.000414} = 0.0203$.

95\% CI: $0.54 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0203 = 0.54 \pm 0.04 = (0.50, 0.58)$.

We are 95\% confident between 50\% and 58\% support the candidate.

content.text:
For small samples, the Agresti-Coull "plus-four" method improves 
coverage:

Add 2 successes and 2 failures to the data, then use:
\[
 \tilde{p} = \frac{X + 2}{n + 4}
\]

Media reports often say a poll has a "margin of error of $\pm 3\%$." 
This typically assumes:
